perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees that; (a) it will use Information disclosed by the Disclosing Party solely for the purpose of discussing and evaluating the Transaction, and for no other purpose; 1 (b) it will not distribute, disclose or disseminate Information to any third Party without the prior consent of the Disclosing Party, and will only distribute, disclose or disseminate Information to its directors, officers, employees consultants, advisors, potential sources of financing or other representatives with a need to know such information for purposes of discussing and evaluating the Transaction and who are informed by such Receiving Party of the confidential nature of the Information; (c) it will protect the Information of the Disclosing Part), against unauthorized disclosure and warrants that will use the same means to protect Information that it uses to protect its own confidential information, in any event no less than reasonable means.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees that; (a) it will use Information disclosed by the Disclosing Party for the purpose of discussing and evaluating the Transaction; (b) it will not distribute, disclose or disseminate Information to any third Party without considering the Disclosing Party's perspective, and may distribute, disclose or disseminate Information to its directors, officers, employees consultants, advisors, potential sources of financing or other representatives who may have a use for such information. (c) it will endeavor to protect the Information of the Disclosing Party against unauthorized disclosure, using means it deems adequate.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces several ambiguities. In (a), removing "solely" loosens the restriction. In (b), replacing 'prior consent' with 'considering the Disclosing Party's perspective' makes the requirement subjective. Replacing 'with a need to know' with 'who may have a use for such information' broadens the permitted recipients. In (c), replacing 'warrants that will use the same means to protect Information that it uses to protect its own confidential information, in any event no less than reasonable means' with 'endeavor to protect the Information of the Disclosing Party against unauthorized disclosure, using means it deems adequate' makes the protection standard subjective and potentially less rigorous.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modifications weaken the obligation to protect confidential information. The Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. The original text, while perhaps still needing improvement, sets a minimum standard of 'no less than reasonable means.' The modified text, by allowing the Receiving Party to use means 'it deems adequate,' could allow for a standard of care lower than 'reasonable,' thus potentially violating the Act if it leads to misappropriation of trade secrets due to inadequate protection.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Maryland Commercial Law Code Section 11-1205 (2023) - Preservation of Trade Secret During Action :: 2023 Maryland Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Maryland Code 2023 Maryland Code (here) 2022 Maryland Code View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2023 Maryland Statutes Commercial Law Title 11 - Trade Regulation Subtitle 12 - Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act Section 11-1205 - Preservation of Trade Secret During Action Universal Citation: MD Commercial Law Code § 11-1205 (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next In an action under this subtitle, a court shall preserve the secrecy of an alleged trade secret by reasonable means, which may include granting protective orders in connection with discovery proceedings, holding in-camera hearings, sealing the records of the action, and ordering any person involved in the litigation not to disclose an alleged trade secret without prior court approval. Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. Maryland may have more current or accurate information. perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguities that weaken the protection of confidential information, potentially contradicting the requirement for 'reasonable means' to preserve trade secrets. The original text provided a minimum standard of 'no less than reasonable means'. The changed text replaces this with 'means it deems adequate,' potentially allowing a standard of care lower than reasonable. This directly contradicts the legal obligation to actively preserve trade secrets. The Law Explanation states that the Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions that 'a court shall preserve the secrecy of an alleged trade secret by reasonable means'. This indicates a legal expectation of reasonable measures for trade secret protection, which the modified text undermines.

perturbation.original_text: Vector agrees that, for a period of three (3) months from the termination or expiration of this Agreement, unless such shall have been specifically invited in writing by SafeNet, neither Vector nor any of the investment funds it manages will in any manner, directly or indirectly: (a) effect to seek, offer or propose (whether publicly or otherwise) to effect, or cause or participate in or in any way assist any other person to effect or seek, offer or propose (whether publicly or otherwise) to effect or participate in, (i) any acquisition of any securities (or beneficial ownership thereof) or assets of SafeNet; (ii) any tender or exchange offer, merger or other business combination involving SafeNet; (iii) any recapitalization, restructuring, liquidation, dissolution or other extraordinary transaction with respect to SafeNet; or (iv) any “solicitation” or “proxies” (as such terms are used in the proxy rules of the Securities and Exchange Commission) or consents to vote any voting securities of SafeNet; (b) form, join or in any way participate in a “group” (as defined under the Securities Exchange Act) relating to SafeNet or its securities; (c) otherwise act, alone or in concert. with others, to seek to control or influence the management, Board of Directors or policies of SafeNet; (d) take any action which might force SafeNet to make a public announcement regarding any of the types of matters set forth in (a) above; or (e) enter into any discussions or arrangements with any third party with respect to any of the foregoing.
perturbation.changed_text: Vector agrees that, for a period of three (3) months from the termination or expiration of this Agreement, unless specifically invited by SafeNet, Vector will generally refrain from: (a) seeking an acquisition of securities or assets of SafeNet, (b) participating in a group relating to SafeNet, (c) acting to influence the management of SafeNet, (d) taking actions that might lead SafeNet to make a public announcement, or (e) entering discussions with third parties. This will only apply to investments that Vector deems potentially harmful to SafeNet.
perturbation.explanation: The revised text introduces ambiguity by using vague terms like "generally refrain", "specifically invited," and "potentially harmful." It also removes the phrase "in writing." This makes the restrictions less clear and more open to interpretation. The addition of the sentence "This will only apply to investments that Vector deems potentially harmful to SafeNet" gives Vector too much leeway, and that term is not defined.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 13(d) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 requires any person or group of persons who acquire beneficial ownership of more than 5% of a class of equity securities of a company to disclose certain information. The original text aims to prevent Vector from circumventing these disclosure requirements by acting in concert with others. The changed text, by using vague terms and limiting the restriction to investments Vector deems 'potentially harmful,' could allow Vector to acquire a significant stake in SafeNet without triggering the disclosure requirements, thus contradicting the intent and purpose of Section 13(d).

Periodical and other reports (a) Reports by issuer of security; contents Every issuer of a security registered pursuant to section 78l of this title shall file with the Commission, in accordance with such rules and regulations as the Commission may prescribe as necessary or appropriate for the proper protection of investors and to insure fair dealing in the security- (1) such information and documents (and suc
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity and gives Vector excessive discretion, potentially allowing it to circumvent disclosure requirements under the Securities Exchange Act. The original text explicitly prohibits specific actions related to acquiring securities or influencing SafeNet. The changed text uses vague terms like 'generally refrain' and 'potentially harmful', weakening the restrictions. By adding the sentence, 'This will only apply to investments that Vector deems potentially harmful to SafeNet,' Vector gains the power to subjectively determine whether an investment is restricted, which undermines the intent of preventing undisclosed accumulation of influence or control over SafeNet. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, under which Section 13(d) exists, supporting the Law Explanation's claim that the changed text contradicts the purpose of that Act.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party represents that, as of the date of this agreement, neither Party nor its controlled affiliates, nor anyone acting on its or their behalf in connection with the Transaction, has acquired record or beneficial ownership of any voting securities of the other Party or of any securities convertible or exchangeable into or exercisable for voting securities of the other Party, and each Party agrees that neither it, nor any of its directors, officers or employees who have been provided with the Information will perform any purchase, sale or other transfer of any of the securities of the other Party in violation of United States securities laws.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party believes that, as of the date of this agreement, neither Party nor its controlled affiliates, nor anyone acting on its or their behalf in connection with the Transaction, has knowingly acquired record or beneficial ownership of any voting securities of the other Party or of any securities convertible or exchangeable into or exercisable for voting securities of the other Party, and each Party agrees that it will encourage its directors, officers or employees who have been provided with the Information to avoid performing any purchase, sale or other transfer of any of the securities of the other Party if such activity violates United States securities laws.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'represents' to 'believes' and adding 'knowingly', the assertion becomes less definitive and harder to prove. Similarly, changing 'agrees that neither it, nor any of its directors, officers or employees...will perform' to 'agrees that it will encourage its directors, officers or employees...to avoid performing' weakens the commitment and makes it non-binding. This introduces an element of subjective interpretation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Insider Trading Rules under Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 10b-5 prohibits the purchase or sale of securities based on material non-public information. The original text creates a direct obligation on the parties and their representatives not to engage in such trading. The modified text weakens this obligation by only requiring the parties to 'encourage' their directors, officers, and employees to avoid insider trading. This does not fulfill their legal obligation to prevent insider trading, which would be considered an act of bad faith, therefore, this violates Rule 10b-5.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The original text imposes a direct obligation to not violate securities laws, while the changed text weakens this obligation by only requiring parties to encourage their representatives to avoid such violations. This contradicts Rule 10b-5, which prohibits securities fraud. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that it is unlawful 'to employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud...in connection with the purchase or sale of any security.' The modified text introduces ambiguity and reduces the enforceability of preventing insider trading, thus creating a potential loophole for actions that would violate Rule 10b-5.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that a Receiving Party is requested pursuant to, or required by, applicable law, regulation or legal process to disclose any of the Disclosing Party’s Information, the Receiving Party will notify the Disclosing Party promptly before such Information is disclosed so that the Disclosing Party may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy or, in the sole reasonable discretion of the Disclosing Party, waive compliance with the terms of this Agreement. In the event that no such protective order or other remedy is obtained, or that such Disclosing Party waives compliance with the terms of this Agreement, the Receiving Party will furnish only that portion of such Information which it is advised in writing by outside counsel is legally required and will exercise its best efforts to obtain reliable assurance that confidential treatment will be accorded such Information.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that a Receiving Party is requested pursuant to, or required by, applicable law, regulation or legal process to disclose any of the Disclosing Party’s Information, the Receiving Party will attempt to notify the Disclosing Party before such Information is disclosed. In the event that no such protective order or other remedy is obtained, or that such Disclosing Party waives compliance with the terms of this Agreement, the Receiving Party will furnish the information it deems necessary, and will try to obtain assurance that confidential treatment will be accorded such Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides for prompt notification and limits disclosure to what outside counsel advises is legally required. The modified text changes 'will notify the Disclosing Party promptly' to 'will attempt to notify the Disclosing Party,' which makes the notification discretionary and less enforceable. Also, the change from 'furnish only that portion of such Information which it is advised in writing by outside counsel is legally required' to 'furnish the information it deems necessary' gives the Receiving Party broad discretion over what to disclose, and replacing 'will exercise its best efforts to obtain reliable assurance' with 'will try to obtain assurance' weakens the effort required to maintain confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 45(d)(3) outlines the protection of persons subject to subpoenas. If a subpoena requires disclosure of privileged or protected matter, a party must assert the privilege and cannot simply disclose the information. The original clause provides a mechanism for the Disclosing Party to seek a protective order. The modified clause weakens the Receiving Party's obligation to protect the Disclosing Party's confidential information when faced with a subpoena, instead of requiring the party to only furnish what their lawyer requires, they furnish what they 'deem necessary.' This directly contradicts the rule that protects privilege.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. If th

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the Receiving Party's obligation to protect the Disclosing Party's confidential information, creating an ambiguity that undermines legal enforceability. The original text mandates 'prompt' notification and limits disclosure to what outside counsel advises is legally required. The changed text makes notification discretionary ('will attempt to notify') and allows the Receiving Party to disclose what it 'deems necessary,' contradicting the principle of protecting privileged information outlined in the Law Explanation. While the snippets provided don't directly address the specific obligations modified, the Law Explanation asserts that this weakening contradicts the rule that protects privilege. The move from 'will exercise its best efforts' to 'will try to obtain assurance' further dilutes the commitment to confidentiality, making the legal obligation ambiguous and less enforceable.

perturbation.original_text: With respect to its directors, officers and employees, the Receiving Party warrants that it has executed confidentiality agreements with all such directors, officers and employees who will have access to Information, and shall ensure that such directors, officers and employees protect Information according to the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: With respect to its directors, officers and employees, the Receiving Party intends to execute confidentiality agreements with directors, officers and employees who may have access to Information, and shall encourage such directors, officers and employees to protect Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'warrants that it has executed confidentiality agreements' to 'intends to execute confidentiality agreements' weakens the guarantee and makes it a statement of intent rather than a binding commitment. Also, changing 'shall ensure that such directors, officers and employees protect Information according to the terms of this Agreement' to 'shall encourage such directors, officers and employees to protect Information' reduces the obligation from ensuring protection to merely encouraging it.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act (MUTSA) - Duty to Maintain Secrecy
perturbation.law_explanation: The Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By only 'intending' to have confidentiality agreements and only 'encouraging' protection, the modified text fails to ensure adequate steps are taken to safeguard confidential information. This could be deemed a failure to take reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, in violation of the Act. The original agreement was stronger because it claimed the Receiving Party 'warrants' the agreement and 'shall ensure' protection, versus the changes to merely 'intends' to make agreements and 'shall encourage' compliance.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Maryland Commercial Law Code Section 11-1203 (2023) - Right to Damages :: 2023 Maryland Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Maryland Code 2023 Maryland Code (here) 2022 Maryland Code View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2023 Maryland Statutes Commercial Law Title 11 - Trade Regulation Subtitle 12 - Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act Section 11-1203 - Right to Damages Universal Citation: MD Commercial Law Code § 11-1203 (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next (a) Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. (b) Damages under this subtitle may include: (1) The actual loss caused by misappropriation; and (2) The unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. (c) In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator’s unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (d) If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection (a) of this section. Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. Maryland may have more current or accurate information. We make no warranties or guarantees about the accuracy, completeness, or adequacy of the information contained on this site or the information linked to on the state site. Please check official sources. This site is protected by reCAPTCHA and the Google Privacy Policy and Terms of Service apply. Free Daily Summaries in Your Inbox You're all set! You already r
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the original contract's commitment to protecting confidential information, potentially violating the Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act. The original text used 'warrants' and 'shall ensure,' which create a strong obligation. The modified text uses 'intends' and 'encourage,' which are significantly weaker and may not meet the standard of 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy, as suggested by the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2 refers to damages for misappropriation under the Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act, implying a legal expectation of reasonable protection measures. The change from ensuring protection to merely encouraging it, coupled with the softened commitment to having confidentiality agreements, creates a contradiction by undermining the legal expectation of safeguarding trade secrets. While Snippet 1 has errors, Snippet 2 supports the potential for damages from trade secret misappropriation, reinforcing the need for a robust effort to maintain secrecy, which the modified text fails to provide.
